subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 12! In this unit you will master ratio and proportion, two of the most useful ideas in all of mathematics. A ratio is a way of comparing quantities, and proportion is what happens when two ratios stay equal as amounts grow or shrink. You already meet these ideas every day, even when nobody names them. When Amina mixes camel milk and water for the family, when Hassan shares the profit from a stall in the Hargeisa market, or when Yusuf reads the scale printed on a map before travelling between Mogadishu and Kismaayo, they are all reasoning with ratio and proportion.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 8 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work through every example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does this matter so much? Proportional reasoning is the mathematics of comparison and scaling, and it appears almost everywhere. Traders use ratios to divide profit in fair shares. Cooks scale recipes up and down. Builders and architects shrink real buildings onto paper using scale. Scientists mix chemicals in fixed ratios. Even currency exchange, when you change shillings into dollars, is a proportion problem. When you become fluent with ratio and proportion, you gain a tool you will use for the rest of your life, and you build the foundation for later topics such as similar triangles, gradients, and linear functions.

As you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Set up each ratio carefully, show every step of your working, and always check that your answer makes sense. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it simply shows you exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
Write ratios in different forms and simplify them to their lowest terms using the highest common factor.
Find equivalent ratios and understand why the order in a ratio matters.
Share an amount in a given ratio, and work backwards when one share is known.
Recognise direct proportion and solve problems with the unitary method and cross multiplication.
Recognise inverse proportion, where one quantity rises as the other falls.
Interpret and use scales to convert between map distances and real distances.
Calculate rates and unit rates, and use them to compare value and solve speed problems.

concepts.title: Understanding and Writing Ratios
concepts.explanation: A ratio compares two or more quantities and shows their relative sizes. If a class at Nuur Academy has 15 boys and 10 girls, the ratio of boys to girls is written 15:10, read aloud as “15 to 10”. Ratios can be written in three ways: with a colon (15:10), as a fraction (15/10), or in words (“15 to 10”). All three mean the same thing. The order of a ratio matters enormously. If the ratio of red to blue marbles is 4:3, that means 4 red for every 3 blue, and it is completely different from 3:4. Always write the parts…
concepts.example: From one situation you can often write several different ratios. In that class of 15 boys and 10 girls, the ratio of boys to girls is 15:10, the ratio of girls to boys is 10:15, and the ratio of boys to the total of 25…

concepts.title: Simplifying Ratios and Equivalent Ratios
concepts.explanation: Just like fractions, a ratio is usually written in its simplest form. To simplify, divide every part by their highest common factor (HCF), the largest number that divides into all the parts exactly. To simplify 12:8, the HCF of 12 and 8 is 4, so 12:8 = (12÷4):(8÷4) = 3:2. A ratio with three parts works the same way. To simplify 15:25:10, the HCF of all three numbers is 5, so 15:25:10 = 3:5:2. Ratios that describe the same relationship are called equivalent ratios. The ratios 2:3, 4:6, 6:9 and 8:12 are all…
concepts.example: To find a missing value in a pair of equivalent ratios, work out the multiplier. In 3:4 = 6:?, the first part has been multiplied by 2 (because 3×2 = 6), so the second part must also be multiplied by 2, giving 4×2 = 8.…

concepts.title: Sharing in a Given Ratio
concepts.explanation: One of the most useful skills in this unit is sharing an amount in a given ratio. The method has three steps: add the parts of the ratio to find the total number of parts, divide the amount by that total to find the value of one part, then multiply to find each share. Let us share $60 in the ratio 2:3. The total parts = 2 + 3 = 5. The value of one part = $60 ÷ 5 = $12. So the first share = 2 × $12 = $24 and the second share = 3 × $12 = $36. Always check: $24 + $36 = $60, which matches, so the sharing is correct.…
concepts.example: Sometimes the amount carries a unit, such as money, mass, or volume, but nothing changes about the method. To share 2.4 kg of dates in the ratio 3:2:1, the total parts = 6, one part = 2.4 ÷ 6 = 0.4 kg, and the shares…

concepts.title: Direct Proportion, the Unitary Method and Cross Multiplication
concepts.explanation: Two quantities are in direct proportion if they increase or decrease at the same rate: when one doubles, the other doubles too; when one halves, the other halves. The cost of dates is directly proportional to the number of kilograms you buy, and the distance a bus travels is directly proportional to the time it drives at a steady speed. In symbols we write y = kx, where k is a fixed number called the constant of proportionality. You can test for direct proportion by checking whether y ÷ x gives the same value…
concepts.example: The unitary method is the most reliable way to solve direct-proportion problems. You find the value of one unit, then scale up to the amount you need. If 5 apples cost $3, then 1 apple costs $3 ÷ 5 = $0.60, so 8 apples…

concepts.title: Inverse Proportion
concepts.explanation: Two quantities are in inverse proportion when one increases as the other decreases, in a balanced way. The classic example is workers and time: the more workers you have, the less time a job takes. In symbols, xy = k, meaning the product of the two quantities stays constant, so y = k/x. Consider a job that 6 workers complete in 8 days. How long would 4 workers take? First find the total amount of work as a product: 6 × 8 = 48 worker-days. This is the constant k. With 4 workers, the time = 48 ÷ 4 = 12 days. Fewer…
concepts.example: Speed and time for a fixed journey are also inversely proportional. If a trip takes 3 hours at 50 km/h, the distance is 150 km, and at 75 km/h the same 150 km takes only 150 ÷ 75 = 2 hours. The faster you go, the less…

concepts.title: Scale and Maps
concepts.explanation: To find a real distance from a map, multiply the map distance by the scale factor. On a map with scale 1:50 000, if two towns are 6 cm apart, the real distance = 6 × 50 000 = 300 000 cm = 3000 m = 3 km. To find a map distance from a real distance, divide instead. If two cities are 15 km apart, first convert 15 km to 1 500 000 cm, then divide by the scale: 1 500 000 ÷ 100 000 = 15 cm on a 1:100 000 map. Scale also lets you make accurate scale drawings. To draw a room that is 4 m by 3 m at scale 1:100, convert the…
concepts.example: One idea that surprises many learners: when lengths scale by a factor, area scales by the factor squared and volume by the factor cubed. If the length scale is 1:10, the area scale is 1:100 and the volume scale is…

methods.title: How to simplify a ratio
steps: Make sure all parts are in the same unit.
Find the highest common factor of every part.
Divide each part by the HCF.
Check that the parts share no common factor other than 1.

methods.title: How to solve a proportion
methods.example: Write 40 cm to 1 m as a ratio in simplest form.
steps: Decide whether the problem is direct or inverse proportion.
For direct: use the unitary method or cross multiplication.
For inverse: keep the product of the two quantities constant.
Check that the answer makes sense (more or less, as expected).

methods.title: How to use a map scale
steps: Read the scale, for example 1:50 000.
To find a real distance, multiply the map distance by the scale.
To find a map distance, divide the real distance by the scale.
Convert the answer into sensible units (cm, m or km).

methods.title: Work backwards from one share
methods.example: Two numbers are in the ratio 3:7. If the smaller is 15, find the larger.
steps: Solution: The smaller number is 3 parts, worth 15, so one part = 15 ÷ 3 = 5.
The larger number is 7 parts = 7 × 5 = 35.
Check the work backwards from one share result carefully.

methods.title: Unitary method
methods.example: If 4 apples cost $3, how much do 10 apples cost?
steps: Solution: Find the cost of one apple: $3 ÷ 4 = $0.75.
Then 10 apples cost 10 × $0.75 = $7.50.
Divide to reach one unit, then multiply to reach ten.

methods.title: Cross multiplication
steps: Solution: Cross multiply: 4 × x = 7 × 12, so 4x = 84 and x = 84 ÷ 4 = 21.
Check: 4:7 and 12:21 both simplify to 4:7.
Check the cross multiplication result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Simplify a ratio
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The HCF of 18 and 24 is 6. Divide both parts by 6: 18÷6 = 3 and 24÷6 = 4. So 18:24 = 3:4. Check that 3 and 4 share no common factor other than 1, which they do not, so the ratio is fully simplified.

workedExamples.title: Convert units, then simplify
workedExamples.prompt: Write 40 cm to 1 m as a ratio in simplest form.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Convert to the same unit first: 1 m = 100 cm. So the ratio is 40:100. The HCF of 40 and 100 is 20, giving 40÷20 = 2 and 100÷20 = 5. The answer is 2:5.

workedExamples.title: Share in a given ratio
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Total parts = 2 + 3 + 5 = 10. One part = $150 ÷ 10 = $15. The shares are 2×$15 = $30, 3×$15 = $45 and 5×$15 = $75. Check: $30 + $45 + $75 = $150. The three shares are $30, $45 and $75.

workedExamples.title: Work backwards from one share
workedExamples.prompt: Two numbers are in the ratio 3:7. If the smaller is 15, find the larger.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The smaller number is 3 parts, worth 15, so one part = 15 ÷ 3 = 5. The larger number is 7 parts = 7 × 5 = 35.

workedExamples.title: Unitary method
workedExamples.prompt: If 4 apples cost $3, how much do 10 apples cost?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the cost of one apple: $3 ÷ 4 = $0.75. Then 10 apples cost 10 × $0.75 = $7.50. Divide to reach one unit, then multiply to reach ten.

workedExamples.title: Cross multiplication
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Cross multiply: 4 × x = 7 × 12, so 4x = 84 and x = 84 ÷ 4 = 21. Check: 4:7 and 12:21 both simplify to 4:7.

workedExamples.title: Inverse proportion
workedExamples.prompt: 6 workers can complete a task in 8 days. How long would 4 workers take?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: This is inverse proportion, so use the constant product. Total work = 6 × 8 = 48 worker-days. Time for 4 workers = 48 ÷ 4 = 12 days. Fewer workers, so more time, which makes sense.

workedExamples.title: Map scale
workedExamples.prompt: On a map with scale 1:50 000, two towns are 4 cm apart. Find the real distance in kilometres.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Real distance = 4 × 50 000 = 200 000 cm. Convert: 200 000 cm = 2000 m = 2 km.

workedExamples.title: Better value with unit rates
workedExamples.prompt: Brand A sells 500 g for $3.50 and Brand B sells 750 g for $5.00. Which is better value?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the price per 100 g. Brand A: $3.50 ÷ 5 = $0.70 per 100 g. Brand B: $5.00 ÷ 7.5 = $0.67 per 100 g. Brand B is cheaper per 100 g, so Brand B is the better value.

workedExamples.title: Speed
workedExamples.prompt: A car travels 240 km in 4 hours. Find its average speed.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Which is better value: 6 pens for $12 or 4 pens for $9? Show the unit price.
workedExamples.solution: 6 for $12 = $2 each; 4 for $9 = $2.25 each; the pack of 6 is better value.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A car travelling at 60 km/h takes 4 hours for a journey. How long would the same journey take at 80 km/h?

activities.title: Recipe Scaling Kitchen
activities.materials: a family recipe, a pencil and paper, and measuring cups or a scale.
steps: Choose a recipe such as bariis or anjero that lists ingredients for a fixed number of people.
Work out the scale factor to feed twice as many people, then three times as many.
Multiply every ingredient by the scale factor and write out the new recipe.
If you can, cook the scaled recipe and check that the proportions still taste right.
Cooking is one of the most common places proportion is used every single day.

activities.title: Mixing Ratios with Drinks
activities.materials: cordial or juice, water, and a measuring cup.
steps: Mix a drink using cordial to water in the ratio 1:4 and taste it.
Now mix a stronger drink in the ratio 1:2 and compare the taste.
Predict how 1:6 will taste, then mix it and check your prediction.
Write down which ratio your family prefers, and explain why in terms of parts.

activities.title: Map and Scale Explorer
activities.materials: any map with a printed scale (a road map, an atlas, or a phone map).
steps: Find the scale printed on the map, for example 1:100 000.
Measure the map distance between two towns such as Hargeisa and Berbera with a ruler.
Use the scale to calculate the real distance in kilometres.
Check your answer against a signpost or an online distance if you can.

activities.title: Best-Value Market Investigation
activities.materials: prices of the same item in different pack sizes (real or from a shop).
steps: Find one item, such as rice or sugar, sold in two or three different pack sizes.
Work out the unit rate (price per kilogram or per 100 g) for each pack.
Decide which pack is the best value using the unit rates.
Discuss with your family whether the biggest pack is always the cheapest per kilogram.

activities.title: The Sharing Game (Family or Group)
activities.materials: a pile of small counters such as dates, beans or stones, and paper.
steps: One player names an amount and a ratio, for example 'share 24 dates in the ratio 3:5'.
Every player works out the shares on paper using the three-step method.
Deal out the real counters to check that the shares are correct and add back to the total.
Take turns setting harder problems, including three-part ratios such as 2:3:1.

activities.title: Golden Ratio Investigation
activities.materials: a ruler and several rectangular objects (a book, a card, a phone).
steps: Measure the length and width of each rectangle and write the ratio length:width.
Divide the longer side by the shorter side to get a single number for each object.
Compare your numbers to the golden ratio, which is about 1.618.
Discuss which shapes look most pleasing and whether their ratio is close to 1.618.

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which activity helped you understand ratio and proportion best? What was the trickiest part? Where in daily life do you now notice ratios and rates? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: You can test for direct proportion by
rules.text: You can test for direct proportion by checking whether y ÷ x gives the same value every time. In the table 2→6, 4→12, 6→18, each y divided by its x gives 3 (6÷2 = 3, 12÷4 = 3, 18÷6 = 3). Because y ÷ x is always 3, the quantities are in direct proportion and the rule is y = 3x. If the graph of the relationship is a straight line through the origin, that is another sign of direct proportion.

rules.title: Cross multiplication is a fast way to
rules.text: Cross multiplication is a fast way to solve a proportion of the form a:b = c:d, where one value is unknown. The rule is a × d = b × c. To solve 3:5 = x:20, cross multiply to get 3 × 20 = 5 × x, so 60 = 5x and x = 12. Here is a shopping example: if 6 pencils cost $4.50, how much do 10 cost? Set up 6:$4.50 = 10:x, so 6x = 4.50 × 10 = 45, giving x = $7.50. Both the unitary method and cross multiplication give the same answer, so use whichever feels clearer for the problem in front of you.

terms: A comparison of two or more quantities
Divide every part by the HCF to lowest terms
Different ratios that describe the same relationship
A statement that two ratios are equal
As one quantity rises, the other rises at the same rate
As one quantity rises, the other falls
Find one unit first, then scale up
The ratio of drawing length to real length
A comparison of different quantities; per one unit
commonMistakes: Match the parts to the order named in the question
Divide every part by the HCF
Add the parts, find one part, then multiply
More gives less means inverse: keep the product constant
Convert everything to the same unit first

realProblems.prompt: A cake recipe for 8 people uses 300 g flour, 200 g sugar and 4 eggs. Maryam wants to bake for 12 people. How much of each ingredient does she need?
realProblems.answer: Scale factor 12÷8 = 1.5, so 450 g flour, 300 g sugar, 6 eggs.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A car travelling at 60 km/h takes 4 hours for a journey. How long would the same journey take at 80 km/h?
The ratio of water to juice is 2:3, and the ratio of juice to syrup is 5:1. Find the ratio water:juice:syrup.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Water:juice = 2:3 and juice:syrup = 5:1; scale juice to 15, giving water:juice:syrup = 10:15:3.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: In a ratio of 3:7, what percentage does each part represent?

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Scale and Maps.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: To find a real distance from a map, multiply the map distance by the scale factor. On a map with scale 1:50 000, if two towns are 6 cm apart, the real distance = 6 × 50 000 = 300 000 cm = 3000 m = 3 km. To find a map distance from a real distance, divide instead. If two cities are 15 km apart, first convert 15 km to 1 500 000 cm, then divide by the scale: 1 500 000 ÷ 100 000 = 15 cm on a 1:100 000 map. Scale also lets you make accurate scale drawings. To draw a room that is 4 m by 3 m at scale 1:100, convert the…

questions.question: Which fraction is equivalent to 1/2?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2, so they are equivalent.

questions.question: What does “Ratio” mean?
options: A comparison of two or more quantities
Divide every part by the HCF to lowest terms
Different ratios that describe the same relationship
A statement that two ratios are equal
questions.answer: A comparison of two or more quantities
questions.hint: Use the Unit 12 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Ratio means A comparison of two or more quantities.

questions.question: What does “Simplify” mean?
options: Divide every part by the HCF to lowest terms
Different ratios that describe the same relationship
A statement that two ratios are equal
As one quantity rises, the other rises at the same rate
questions.answer: Divide every part by the HCF to lowest terms
questions.hint: Use the Unit 12 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Simplify means Divide every part by the HCF to lowest terms.

questions.question: What does “Equivalent ratios” mean?
options: Different ratios that describe the same relationship
A statement that two ratios are equal
As one quantity rises, the other rises at the same rate
As one quantity rises, the other falls
questions.answer: Different ratios that describe the same relationship
questions.hint: Use the Unit 12 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Equivalent ratios means Different ratios that describe the same relationship.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work through every example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does this matter so much? Proportional reasoning is the mathematics of comparison and scaling, and it appears almost everywhere. Traders use ratios to divide profit in fair shares. Cooks scale recipes up and down. Builders and architects shrink real buildings onto paper using scale. Scientists mix chemicals in fixed ratios. Even currency exchange, when you change shillings into dollars, is a proportion problem. When you become fluent with ratio and proportion, you gain a tool you will use for the rest of your life, and you build the foundation for later topics such as similar triangles, gradients, and linear functions.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Set up each ratio carefully, show every step of your working, and always check that your answer makes sense. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it simply shows you exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
I can write ratios in different forms and simplify them to their lowest terms using the highest common factor.
I can find equivalent ratios and understand why the order in a ratio matters.
I can share an amount in a given ratio, and work backwards when one share is known.
I can recognise direct proportion and solve problems with the unitary method and cross multiplication.
I can recognise inverse proportion, where one quantity rises as the other falls.
